questions.q: What is back-EMF in a DC motor?
options: A current that flows backwards through the supply
An induced EMF (voltage) in the motor's own coil that opposes the supply voltage
A force that pushes the coil backwards
The resistance of the armature windings
questions.explanation: Back-EMF is an induced EMF — a voltage, in volts — produced in the motor's rotating coil that acts opposite to the supply voltage. It is not a current and not a force; those are downstream consequences. Calling it a current or force is a classic mark-loser.

questions.q: Which two laws explain the production of back-EMF, in the correct order?
options: Ohm's law, then Newton's law
Faraday's law produces the EMF; Lenz's law makes it oppose the supply
Lenz's law produces the EMF; Faraday's law makes it oppose
Coulomb's law, then Kirchhoff's law
questions.explanation: The rotating coil has a changing flux, so by Faraday's law an EMF is induced; by Lenz's law that induced EMF opposes the change driving it, so it opposes the supply. "Faraday makes it, Lenz flips it."

questions.q: Why must the back-EMF oppose the supply rather than aid it?
options: Because the armature resistance demands it
Because of conservation of energy — otherwise the motor would accelerate without limit, creating energy from nothing
Because the field magnets are fixed
Because the commutator reverses it
questions.explanation: If the back-EMF aided the supply, the motor would speed up endlessly and produce energy from nothing — a perpetual motion machine. Lenz's law (the induced EMF opposes the change) is conservation of energy in disguise, so the back-EMF must oppose the supply.

questions.q: How does the back-EMF depend on the rotational speed of the motor?
options: It decreases as the motor speeds up
It is independent of speed
It increases as the motor speeds up, because the flux changes more rapidly
It is largest when the motor is stationary
questions.explanation: By Faraday's law the induced EMF is proportional to the rate of change of flux. A faster coil changes its flux more rapidly, so the back-EMF rises with speed. At zero speed the flux is not changing, so the back-EMF is exactly zero.

questions.q: The current through a DC motor is given by which relationship?
options: \(I = \dfrac{V_{\text{supply}}}{R}\)
\(I = \dfrac{V_{\text{supply}} - V_{\text{back}}}{R}\)
\(I = \dfrac{V_{\text{back}}}{R}\)
\(I = \dfrac{V_{\text{supply}} + V_{\text{back}}}{R}\)
questions.explanation: Only the net voltage (supply minus back-EMF) drives current through the armature resistance, so \(I = (V_{\text{supply}} - V_{\text{back}})/R\). Using the bare supply voltage gives the start-up current only — that is the most common error.

questions.q: A motor with armature resistance 10 Ω runs from a 240 V supply. At start-up (back-EMF = 0), what is the current?
questions.explanation: At start-up the back-EMF is zero, so the net voltage is the full 240 V: \(I = 240/10 = 24\ \mathrm{A}\). This large surge occurs because no back-EMF exists until the coil moves.

questions.q: The same 10 Ω, 240 V motor runs normally with a back-EMF of 232 V. What is the running current?
options: 8 A
questions.explanation: Net voltage \(= 240 - 232 = 8\ \mathrm{V}\), so \(I = 8/10 = 0.8\ \mathrm{A}\). The running current is far smaller than the 24 A start-up surge because the large back-EMF cancels most of the supply.

questions.q: How does back-EMF make a motor self-regulating when the mechanical load increases?
options: The load speeds the motor up, raising back-EMF and lowering current
The load slows the motor, lowering back-EMF, raising the net voltage and current, which raises torque
The load has no effect on the current
The motor switches off to protect itself
questions.explanation: Extra load slows the motor, so the back-EMF falls, the net voltage and current rise, and via \(F = BIL\) the larger current produces more torque — exactly when the load demands it. This automatic feedback is why a drill bites harder hitting a knot.

questions.q: Why can a stalled DC motor burn out?
options: The back-EMF becomes very large, overheating the coil
With zero speed the back-EMF is zero, so the current stays at its maximum continuously, and \(I^2R\) heating cooks the windings
The commutator stops reversing, cutting all current
The field magnets demagnetise
questions.explanation: A stalled rotor has zero speed, so zero back-EMF; the net voltage returns to the full supply and the current sits at its maximum continuously. The \(I^2R\) heating has nowhere to go, so the insulation breaks down and the motor burns out. Stall and start-up are the same physics — both have zero back-EMF — but stall is permanent.

questions.q: What device is commonly used to limit the large start-up current of a large DC motor?
options: A capacitor in parallel with the coil
A series starting resistor, switched out once the motor speeds up
An extra set of slip rings
A second commutator
questions.explanation: A series starting resistor cuts the start-up current to a safe value during the brief moment before back-EMF exists. Once the motor spins up and its back-EMF limits the current, the resistor is switched out so it does not waste energy.
